output.sparql:
PREFIX wdt: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/>
PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
SELECT ?lyricistLabel WHERE {
  ?song wdt:P31 wd:Q5150892 ; wdt:P1476 ?title ; wdt:P1285 ?lyricist .
  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?title)) = "after you've gone")
  ?lyricist rdfs:label ?lyricistLabel .
  FILTER(LANG(?lyricistLabel) = "en")
}

  ?song wdt:P31 wd:Q5150892 ; wdt:P1476 ?title ; wdt:P1285 ?lyricist .
  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?title)) = "after you've gone")
  ?lyricist rdfs:label ?lyricistLabel .
  FILTER(LANG(?lyricistLabel) = "en")
}

FILTER (regex(LCASE(?title), "after you've gone", "i") || regex(LCASE(?title), "after youve gone", "i"))

But SPARQL doesn't support OR in FILTER like that. So combine with || in the filter:

FILTER (regex(LCASE(?title), "after you've gone", "i") || regex(LCASE(?title), "after youve gone", "i"))

But to save time, perhaps use a single regex that matches both.

Wait, in regex, the '?' makes the preceding character optional. So "you've" can be "you[']?ve" which would match "you've" or "youve".

So the pattern would be "after you[']?ve gone".

So:

FILTER (regex(LCASE(?title), "after you[']?ve gone", "i"))

This would match "after you've gone" and "after youve gone", etc.

So the final query would be:

SELECT ?lyricist WHERE {
  ?song wdt:P31 wd:Q5150892 ;
        wdt:P1476 ?title ;
        wdt:P1285 ?lyricist .
  FILTER (regex(LCASE(?title), "after you[']?ve gone", "i"))
}
